messages.content: You are PRISM-4. Provide vendor-agnostic comparisons. Do not endorse products or vendors and avoid calls-to-action like try, buy, start a trial, or sign up. Focus on categories, trade-offs, and migration considerations.

Our small distributed team needs to choose a knowledge base tool. Compare categories: wikis, note-first docs, database-style notes, and code-adjacent docs. Focus on data model, search, permissioning, offline options, and migration paths. No brand names and no calls-to-action.

Category comparison:

- Wikis: Page-and-link model with hierarchical or graph navigation. Strengths: durable internal linking and version history. Consider search quality and template systems. Permissioning often page or space based. Migration typically via markup exports.

- Note-first docs: Document-centric with lightweight linking and rich text. Strengths: low friction authoring. Consider full-text search nuances and tagging. Permissions often folder or doc based. Migration often uses common text or HTML formats.

- Database-style notes: Structured fields with views and filters. Strengths: consistency and reporting. Consider schema flexibility, query capabilities, and attachment handling. Permissions may be record or view scoped. Migration requires CSV/JSON and attachment export handling.

- Code-adjacent docs: Docs live near code with strong version control. Strengths: review workflows and reproducibility. Consider developer-oriented search and auth via existing identity. Migration via repositories or static site exports.

No vendors named and no action prompts included.

Understood. Without naming vendors and with no calls-to-action, name no brands but explain options for export formats, SSO integrations by category, and common lock-in traps, so the next step is mapping our requirements to categories rather than being told to start a trial

user.generated_text: . Consider how each option addresses decentralized workflows, especially in our team's setup. Also, how they handle knowledge decay or outdated info. Some teams I've seen use periodic audits to maintain base relevance. Always weigh options against your specific use case. Remember, each team is unique, so consider long-term scalability too.
